items.text:
End-of-Semester Course Evaluations

All students are asked to complete their course evaluations before Friday, December 12, at 11:59 PM. Evaluations are accessed through the student portal under the 'My Courses' tab, and each survey takes about ten minutes.

Please note the following guidelines:
- Evaluations must be submitted separately for every enrolled course.
- Responses are anonymous and will not be shared with instructors until final grades are posted.
- Students who finish all evaluations by December 5 will be entered into a raffle for a $25 campus bookstore gift card.
- Paper forms are available in Room 118 of Halsey Hall for students without reliable internet access.

Feedback is reviewed by department chairs and used to improve course design for the spring semester. Late submissions cannot be accepted once the portal closes.

Questions may be directed to the Office of Academic Assessment at assessment@university.edu or 555-0142.

questions.stem: According to the notice, what are students without reliable internet access told to do?

options.A: Collect a printed form from Room 118 in Halsey Hall.
options.B: Email their responses to the Office of Academic Assessment.
options.C: Log in to the student portal from a campus computer lab.
options.D: Combine all their courses into a single evaluation.

questions.explanation: The notice states that paper forms are available in Room 118 of Halsey Hall for students who lack reliable internet access. Option B invents an email submission route; C reuses 'student portal' but the point is that these students cannot rely on it; D reverses the rule that evaluations must be submitted separately for every course.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the course evaluations?

options.A: They are shared with instructors before final grades are posted.
options.B: They influence how courses are taught in the following term.
options.C: They are required only from students in their first year.
options.D: They have to be completed during scheduled class time.

questions.explanation: The notice says feedback is used to improve course design for the spring semester, implying the evaluations shape future teaching. A reverses the stated timing (responses are withheld until grades are posted); C introduces a first-year restriction not mentioned; D adds a class-time requirement that does not appear in the text.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this notice?

options.A: To advertise a raffle for a campus bookstore gift card.
options.B: To explain how final grades will be calculated this term.
options.C: To remind students to complete course evaluations by the deadline.
options.D: To describe changes to the spring semester course schedule.

questions.explanation: The notice's overall aim is to prompt students to finish their course evaluations before the portal closes. A captures only one detail (the raffle); B and D describe topics the notice never addresses.

items.text:
Vending Machine Refund Procedure

Students who lose money in any campus vending machine may request a refund through the Facilities Services office. This notice explains the steps that must be followed.

Refund requests are handled at the Facilities Services desk in Building C, Room 210, open Monday through Friday from 8:00 AM to 5:00 PM. Please bring the following:
- The exact location and machine number, printed on a sticker near the coin slot.
- The amount lost, which may not exceed $3.00 per request.
- A valid student identification card.

Refunds are issued in cash once the claim is verified, usually within two business days. Amounts under $1.00 are credited to your student account instead of being paid in cash.

Machines are serviced every Wednesday, and repeated faults should be reported so that repairs can be scheduled promptly.

For questions, contact Facilities Services at 555-0198.

questions.stem: According to the notice, what must a student bring to request a refund?

options.A: A receipt printed by the vending machine.
options.D: A written note from a course instructor.

questions.explanation: The notice lists a valid student identification card among the items to bring. A sounds plausible but no receipt is mentioned; C invents an online form when refunds are handled at a desk; D introduces an instructor's note not required anywhere in the text.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about a lost amount of $0.75?

options.A: It is paid in cash within two business days.
options.B: It requires written approval from a department chair.
options.C: It is added to the student's account rather than paid in cash.
options.D: It cannot be claimed because the sum is too small.

questions.explanation: Since amounts under $1.00 are credited to the student account instead of cash, a $0.75 loss would be credited. A reverses that rule; B introduces an approval step not mentioned; D wrongly assumes small amounts are ineligible, though the notice sets only an upper limit of $3.00.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this notice?

options.A: To announce new vending machines installed in Building C.
options.B: To list the snacks available in campus vending machines.
options.C: To warn students against using out-of-order machines.
options.D: To explain how students can recover money lost in vending machines.

questions.explanation: The notice's central aim is to describe the procedure for reclaiming money lost in a vending machine. A and B concern topics the notice never covers; C captures only a minor point about reporting faults, not the main purpose.
